family_audit: accepted_primary: register-pressure reduction on the active PTX hot-band microkernel
accepted_secondary: PTX export-surface cleanup that stays on the live branch
accepted_restore: historical PTX grouping-window restore kept live as a fallback family
deferred: another near-neighbor PTX copy-order/control tweak after the round-5 PASS_FLAT result
rejected_primary: the 256x128 pivot family remains negative for this loop

directions.name: Flatten PTX Hot-Band Compute Helpers To Reduce Register Pressure
directions.hypothesis: The round-5 control-path tweak effectively held runtime flat while leaving the same occupancy-limited signature in place: the hot PTX kernel still reports only 16.49% active warps and the same register-limited CTA ceiling. That makes register pressure the most coherent next family. The historically rehydrated helper-flattening branch already showed a 24.696832 ms run, and the current profile still supports its thesis that the PTX helper structure is keeping too much live state around the active 128x128 hot-band path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and weak latency hiding caused by helper-induced live ranges in the PTX hot-band compute path.
directions.risk: Medium. Helper flattening is still bounded to the active PTX branch, but it can devolve into a no-op if ptxas keeps the same live ranges and scheduling.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: The latest flat result specifically weakens copy-order retunes and strengthens the case for attacking the unchanged register ceiling directly.
This family is already the highest-ranked rehydrated representative in the live frontier after the still-open PTX control family.
It is ranked first because it is the most defensible next move that still stays on the active PTX branch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if register count and active-warps remain pinned after one bounded helper-flattening pass, or if runtime still behaves like noise around the current flat plateau.

directions.name: Apply Only A Minimal PTX Export Address Cleanup
directions.hypothesis: If the helper-lifetime thesis is still too broad, the next lower-risk active-branch move is to keep the PTX hot-band compute path intact and simplify only the export address/control surface. The current run still did not improve barrier or scoreboard behavior, so a smaller export-side cleanup is a credible fallback that stays on the same hot branch while reducing pointer and scratch-management overhead around the final store path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-side address/control overhead and scratch management after MMA, rather than another feed-order or traversal issue.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This is one of the safest remaining PTX-local families, but its upside is smaller than dir_01 because it touches only the export surface.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: This keeps one lower-risk PTX-local path alive after the copy-order retune failed to move the main counters.
It is ranked behind dir_01 because export cleanup alone is less likely to relieve the unchanged occupancy ceiling.
The family was explicitly rehydrated from round_history to keep an auditable active-branch fallback in the queue.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if export cleanup leaves barrier, short-scoreboard, and register pressure effectively unchanged after one bounded pass.

directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: The current loop should keep one restore-style family active instead of repeatedly tunneling on the latest PTX-local micro-tunes. The best historical grouping-window restore still measured 24.444416 ms, close enough to the accepted base to remain meaningful, and it gives the search an auditable locality fallback if both the helper-flattening and export-cleanup families stall.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and launch-order mapping on the accepted PTX surface, but explicitly as a restore fallback rather than the primary next attack.
directions.risk: Low to medium. The family has historical evidence, but the latest fresh grouped traversal probe also makes it a weaker primary bet than the two PTX-local cleanup families.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base and the historical 24.444416 ms restore run
ranking_notes: This is intentionally a restore fallback so the live queue keeps one near-best historical locality family active.
It is ranked below the PTX-local cleanup ideas because the latest fresh locality probe came back flat-to-negative.
The user explicitly asked for round-history-driven queue recovery, and this family is one of the strongest recovered candidates.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the restore cannot recover near its historical behavior or if locality metrics stay flat enough to make the family redundant.

notes: Round 6/20 audit: the round-5 B-first prologue staging tweak measured 24.535040 ms, only 0.015872 ms slower than the previous run, but it moved the wrong counters: active warps slipped from 16.60% to 16.49%, barrier rose from 5.18% to 5.33%, and long-scoreboard rose from 7.28% to 7.50% while registers stayed pinned at the same occupancy ceiling. That is strong evidence that another copy-order micro-tune is not the best immediate use of budget. The diagnosis therefore promotes the rehydrated register-pressure family to rank 1, keeps a narrower PTX export cleanup as the active-branch fallback, and preserves one historical PTX grouping-window restore as the restore-style fallback family. This uses the live queue expansion from round_history instead of letting the loop collapse back to only one or two families.
